title: Barn Door Bookcase
summary: A double-wide bookcase with a full-size sliding barn door on the front — hidden storage behind hardware-mounted, real sliding door track.
description:
This bookcase is really two half-bookcases joined into one wide case, fronted by a genuine sliding barn door on real hardware rather than a hinged cabinet door — a much larger reveal than a typical cabinet, since a barn door needs open wall space beside the case to slide into.

Build one half-bookcase completely first, using a scrap spacer block to keep consistent shelf spacing as you work from the top down, then build and attach the second half to it. The 2x2 face frame legs need spacer blocks of their own during assembly, since the 1x boards and 2x2s are different actual thicknesses and won't sit flush without something to reference against.

The barn door itself is a separate assembly: pocket-holed 1x4 frame, skinned with 1/4" plywood on the back, cut from two different-width plywood strips so the door's back panel is pieced together rather than needing a single oversized sheet. **Hardware and hanging are the step to get right** — the door has to hang plumb, roll smoothly on its track, and fully clear the case opening at both ends of its travel, so dry-fit and test the slide before considering the case finished. Confirm the case is square before adding the back panel — a case that's slightly out of parallel will bind the door no matter how well the door itself is built.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Long point to short point, both ends cut at 45°, ends parallel.

steps.title: Build the first half-bookcase
steps.body: Cut the 1x12 boards using a compound miter saw. Drill three 3/4" pocket holes on each end of all shelf boards, and one 3/4" pocket hole on each end of the 1x2 shelf trim pieces. Build one half of the bookcase.

steps.title: Space and add the remaining shelves
steps.body: Cut a scrap wood spacer block to 12" and use it to continue attaching shelves working top-down, keeping pocket holes on the underside of each shelf.

steps.title: Build and attach the second half
steps.body: Repeat the process to build the second half of the bookcase, then attach it to the first half.

steps.title: Build the face frame
steps.body: On a flat, level surface, build the face frame from the legs, rails, and center stile. Use scrap 1x boards as spacers to align the 1x pieces with the thicker 2x2 legs, since they're different thicknesses.

steps.title: Attach the face frame, top, and back
steps.body: Confirm the bookcase is square, adjusting if needed. Attach the face frame to the front with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails, and the 2x2 legs to the bookcase sides with 2" trim screws. Attach the top with glue, brad nails, and a few 2" screws. Attach the back panel with glue and brad nails, using the case's square factory edge as your reference.

steps.title: Build the barn door
steps.body: Build the door frame with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, including the diagonal brace. Cut the plywood back panel into two strips (one 23" wide, one 25" wide) to fit the door and case back, trimming the 23" piece to length. Attach the door's back panel with brad nails and glue.

steps.title: Hang the door and finish
steps.body: Install the barn door hardware to the door and the cabinet header. Dry-fit and test that the door hangs plumb, rolls smoothly, and clears the opening at both ends of travel before considering the case complete. Sand and finish the whole piece.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

unresolvedImages.alt: Wide bookcase with a sliding barn door on the front
